content: Blockchain records transactions without a central bank by using a decentralized network of computers called nodes. Instead of relying on one trusted authority, every transaction is verified and recorded by multiple participants across this network. When someone makes a transaction, it’s grouped with others into a “block.” This block is then broadcast to all nodes, which independently check its validity based on agreed rules. Once a majority agrees the block is legitimate, it gets added to a chain of previous blocks—hence the name “blockchain.” This chain is stored on every node, making it nearly impossible to alter past transactions without being detected. Because no single entity controls the ledger, the system doesn’t need a central bank to oversee or approve transactions. While it may sound complex, this distributed verification provides transparency and security, reducing the risk of fraud or manipulation that can happen in centralized systems.
